name: Nature
challenge: Outside or by a window, observe three living things (plants or animals). Tap when you have looked carefully.

stories.title: Leaf Detective
stories.reflection: What did you notice about the leaf shape or colour?

pages.text: Layan used a magnifier to observe a leaf up close.
She saw tiny lines called veins, like roads on a map.
Leaves need sunlight and water to help the plant stay healthy.
Year 3 maths link: the leaf might be about six centimetres long — estimate first, then check.

stories.title: Rain And Roots
stories.reflection: Why do plants need water moving through roots and stems?

pages.text: Rain soaked the soil and the roots drank slowly.
The stem carried water upward so the flower could grow taller.
This is part of how living things depend on their habitat.
Weather changes the temperature and shapes the local environment for plants and animals.
If you walk the edge of a small garden bed, you are thinking about perimeter in the real world.

questions.question: Which leaf is usually long and thin like a needle?
questions.explain: Pine trees have leaves shaped like thin needles.
questions.shortcut: Picture a Christmas tree leaf: long and skinny.

questions.question: Which part of a plant often grows under the soil?
questions.explain: Roots grow underground and drink water from soil.
questions.shortcut: R in roots = R in underground 'root room'.

questions.question: Roughly how many millimetres are in 1 centimetre?
questions.explain: One centimetre is made of 10 tiny millimetres.
